items.headline: Major commercial project approved on West San Carlos Street
items.summary: A $696,570 commercial/industrial development is underway at 1300 W San Carlos St, one of San José's key commercial corridors. This represents significant business investment in the area.

items.headline: Cupertino launches business license amnesty program for shopping centers
items.summary: The city is offering amnesty to businesses that haven't properly licensed themselves, along with outreach to shopping centers and new incentives for retail and startups. This could affect local business compliance and shopping district vitality across Cupertino.

items.headline: Great American Litter Pick-up designated as annual city event
items.summary: The City Council approved the Great American Litter Pick-up as an official annual City-sponsored special event and authorized city funding. The city can now accept donations of materials and services for this cleanup initiative.

items.headline: Federal housing grants available for community development
items.summary: The Housing and Human Services Commission reviewed applications for Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) funding on March 25. These federal funds support affordable housing and community development projects in Sunnyvale.

items.headline: San Antonio Road area plan to study new land use and transportation options
items.summary: The Planning Commission is beginning a study session on potential changes to land use and transportation in the San Antonio Road area. This could affect how the area develops and how residents move through it in the coming years.

items.headline: New retail policies aim to revitalize Palo Alto shopping districts
items.summary: The Commission is considering amendments to zoning and building codes to implement retail vitality policies from the city's Comprehensive Plan. The changes are designed to support and strengthen retail areas across Palo Alto.

items.headline: Campbell updates beekeeping rules with new ordinance
items.summary: The city council adopted an updated beekeeping ordinance on March 24, which will set new requirements for residents keeping bees in Campbell. This could affect anyone interested in backyard beekeeping or urban agriculture.

items.headline: Three council members recuse from transit development debate
items.summary: During a hearing on Senate Bill 79 (Transit-Oriented Development), three council members recused themselves due to property conflicts within the affected transit zone, requiring an unusual legal procedure called the "rule of necessity" to proceed. This signals potential significant zoning or development changes near transit areas that could affect property values and neighborhood character.
